entries.prompt: Thinking about the news that your friends, family and acquaintances post or send you online about political and social issues, overall, do you think the {} (Options: Represents just one side, Represents more than one side, They don't send me news about political and social issues, Refused)
question_paraphrased: What's the balance of perspectives in the political news shared by people you know online? (Options: Represents just one side, Represents more than one side, They don't send me news about political and social issues, Refused)
implicit_questions: When you scroll through political posts from your social circle, how often do you encounter an opinion that fundamentally challenges the general consensus of your feed?
In your recent online interactions, can you recall an instance where two people in your network shared articles taking completely opposite stances on the same social issue?
When you check your messages or social feed, to what extent is the content focused on personal updates rather than current events or policy debates?
How comfortable do you feel discussing the specific nature of the information your private network shares with you?
topic_fg: news, social media, data, privacy: news and social media

entries.input_attribute: I do not have much trust in the accuracy of the news and information I get from political leaders and public officials.
entries.original_question: How much do you trust the accuracy of the news and information that you get from political leaders and public officials
entries.subject: accuracy of the news and information that you get from political leaders and public officials
entries.prompt: How much do you trust the {} (Options: A great deal, Some, Not much, Not at all, Refused)
question_paraphrased: How reliable do you find the information shared by politicians and government figures? (Options: A great deal, Some, Not much, Not at all, Refused)
implicit_questions: When a high-ranking official makes a statement about a new policy or event, how often do you find that their facts align perfectly with reality without needing further verification?
How frequently do you find yourself assuming that a statement from a public official is intentionally misleading or completely fabricated?
Think about the last time you heard an update from a public official; did you feel it was generally true but perhaps required a bit of cross-checking?
When you hear a politician speak on the news, do you instinctively feel the need to look for a non-governmental source to see what they might be leaving out?
Do you feel that your perspective on government transparency is too complex or private to be captured by a simple scale of trust?
topic_fg: news, social media, data, privacy: news and social media

entries.input_attribute: I believe that the freedom to publish and access satire should be protected under all circumstances.
entries.original_question: Which comes closer to your view about the following type of news and information? Satire about an issue or event
entries.subject: Satire about an issue or event
entries.prompt: Which comes closer to your view about the following type of news and information? {} (Options: The freedom to publish and access it should be protected, Steps should be taken to restrict it, Refused)
entries.target: The freedom to publish and access it should be protected
question_paraphrased: How do you think we should handle news satire and parody regarding current events? (Options: The freedom to publish and access it should be protected, Steps should be taken to restrict it, Refused)
implicit_questions: When you see a comedian or website making fun of a serious political event, do you feel it is an essential form of free expression even if it is misleading?
Do you worry that satirical stories are often mistaken for real news and contribute to public confusion or harm?
Is your stance on the regulation of humor and parody in the media something you generally keep to yourself?
topic_fg: news, social media, data, privacy: news and social media

entries.input_attribute: I believe that technology platforms have a responsibility to filter out misinformation to prevent the spread of false claims.
entries.original_question: Thinking about news and information online, including social media which comes closer to your own view?
entries.subject: news and information online, including social media
entries.prompt: Thinking about {} which comes closer to your own view? (Options: Technology companies should take steps to restrict false information online, People's freedom to publish and access information should be protected, Refused)
entries.target: Technology companies should take steps to restrict false information online
question_paraphrased: When it comes to the internet and social media, do you think tech companies should limit fake news or should free speech be the priority? (Options: Technology companies should take steps to restrict false information online, People's freedom to publish and access information should be protected, Refused)
implicit_questions: When you encounter a widely shared post that contains objectively incorrect facts, do you feel that the platform should have flagged it before you saw it?
Are you hesitant to share your personal stance on the balance between internet regulation and free expression?
Do you worry more about seeing inaccurate information online or about a company having the power to decide what you are allowed to read?
topic_fg: news, social media, data, privacy: news and social media
political issues: government control

entries.input_attribute: I am convinced the news media will have a very negative impact on solving the country's biggest problems.
entries.original_question: In the future, what kind of an impact do you think the news media will have in solving the biggest problems facing the country?
entries.subject: news media will have in solving the biggest problems
entries.prompt: In the future, what kind of an impact do you think the {} facing the country? (Options: A very positive impact, A somewhat positive impact, A somewhat negative impact, A very negative impact, Refused)
question_paraphrased: Looking ahead, how much do you think the news media will help or hinder the process of fixing our country's major issues? (Options: A very positive impact, A somewhat positive impact, A somewhat negative impact, A very negative impact, Refused)
implicit_questions: To what extent do you feel that current investigative journalism effectively holds power to account and informs the public on constructive solutions?
Do you feel that modern news reporting actively deepens social divisions and spreads misinformation to the point of stalling progress?
When you follow the news, how often do you find information that helps you understand local issues better, even if the overall media landscape is messy?
How often do you feel that news coverage focuses more on sensationalism and conflict rather than providing the context needed to resolve issues?
How comfortable do you feel predicting the long-term societal influence of complex institutions like the press?
topic_fg: news, social media, data, privacy: news and social media
future

entries.input_attribute: I have seen or heard reports about environmental research scientists in the news lately.
entries.original_question: Is what you know about environmental research scientists because you have heard or read about this in the news
entries.subject: environmental research scientists
entries.prompt: Is what you know about {} because you have heard or read about this in the news (Options: Yes, have heard or read about this in the news, No, have not heard or read about this in the news, Refused)
entries.target: Yes, have heard or read about this in the news
question_paraphrased: Did you get your information about environmental researchers from news outlets or media reports? (Options: Yes, have heard or read about this in the news, No, have not heard or read about this in the news, Refused)
implicit_questions: When you think about environmental scientists, can you recall specific headlines or broadcast segments where their work was discussed?
How comfortable do you feel discussing the specific media sources or personal channels through which you learn about scientific professionals?
If you look back at the news you've consumed recently, how often do stories about the daily findings of environmental researchers appear in your feed?

entries.subject: nutrition research scientists
entries.prompt: Is what you know about {} because you have heard or read about this in the news (Options: Yes, have heard or read about this in the news, No, have not heard or read about this in the news, Refused)
entries.target: Yes, have heard or read about this in the news
question_paraphrased: Did you pick up your information regarding nutrition scientists from news reports or articles? (Options: Yes, have heard or read about this in the news, No, have not heard or read about this in the news, Refused)
implicit_questions: Can you recall a time recently where you sat down to read a newspaper or watch a broadcast specifically covering food science and nutrition?
When you think about clinical studies on diet, do the details usually come from a headline you saw or a segment on a news program?
